rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is clear disaster-risk reduction/resilience. The parent statement explicitly targets climate-linked hazards: "cope with larger typhoons and more frequent heavy rains." The specific action is adaptation planning/DRR: "Provide support for municipalities working on risk assessment to prevent damage caused by broken retaining walls for residential land." The primary gate failure (missing token) appears to be a false negative; keep under Adaptation.
policy_statement: TMG will support municipalities' risk assessment efforts to prevent damage from broken residential land retaining walls.
mechanism_description: Provide support for municipalities’ risk assessment work on residential land retaining walls to help prevent damage from retaining wall failures.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (e.g., extreme rainfall/slope saturation) → ↓ exposure/vulnerability via risk assessment + targeted remediation → ↓ landslide/structural failure damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system: Municipalities improve identification, monitoring, and prioritization of at-risk residential land retaining walls (inventories, inspections, hazard mapping, and follow-up actions).  
(2) Mediating process: Engineering/geotechnical assessment detects instability and failure modes (drainage issues, soil saturation, structural deterioration), enabling preventative reinforcement, drainage improvements, or replacement before triggering events.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced probability and severity of landslide/retaining-wall collapse during extreme precipitation, lowering injury risk, property damage, and infrastructure/service disruption (i.e., improved resilience to climate-amplified hazards).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure and vulnerability of people and infrastructure to rainfall-triggered slope/retaining-wall failures, lowering disaster damages and service disruption.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on disaster-risk management—assessing and addressing retaining-wall failure risk—so the primary effect is reduced climate hazard impact (adaptation).  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: It does not primarily change energy use, materials throughput, or carbon sinks; emissions effects (e.g., avoided reconstruction) are secondary and not the main intended pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: While repairs can have embodied emissions and avoided-rebuild benefits, these are ancillary; the mechanism’s intent and largest climate-relevant benefit remain risk reduction from climate-sensitive precipitation/landslide hazards.  
4) Applicability to all instances: Any policy that “supports risk assessment work on retaining walls to prevent failure damage” consistently functions as resilience/risk reduction regardless of city context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., “critical infrastructure resilience,” “landslide risk management,” “planning/assessment”) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level review.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a specific policy were framed primarily as GHG mitigation via reduced rebuild emissions (unusual for retaining-wall assessment programs), categorization could shift; however, for this canonical mechanism the dominant pathway is hazard damage reduction (adaptation).
key_indicators: "Provide support"; "municipalities"; "risk assessment"; "prevent damage"; "broken retaining walls for residential land"
co_benefits: Reduced non-climate geotechnical failure risk; improved public safety and asset management
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an incentive-program if the “support” is explicitly financial (grants/subsidies), but the excerpt does not specify funding vs technical assistance, so it is treated as an enabling planning/strategy instrument.
